content.text: Structured Concurrency with TaskGroup in Python's asyncio

Managing multiple concurrent tasks is one of the most error-prone parts of async programming. Without a disciplined approach, spawned tasks can leak silently, exceptions can get swallowed, and cancellation can leave your program in an inconsistent state. Structured concurrency is the design principle that solves all three of these problems at once, and
is Python's built-in mechanism for applying it.

is a programming paradigm that enforces a strict parent-child relationship between concurrent tasks. Just like a function call must return before the calling code continues, a group of tasks must all complete before the code that spawned them moves forward. Tasks cannot outlive the scope that created them.
is the Python implementation of this idea, introduced in
. It is an asynchronous context manager that lets you spawn multiple coroutines as tasks, waits for all of them to finish, and handles exceptions and cancellation correctly by design.

async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
    task1 = tg.create_task(coroutine_one())
    task2 = tg.create_task(coroutine_two())
# Both tasks are guaranteed to be done here
works, it is not truly structured — tasks can escape their scope, exception handling is inconsistent, and cancellation semantics are complex to reason about.
Async programs are inherently harder to reason about than sequential ones because multiple things happen simultaneously. Three specific problems plagued pre-3.11 async Python:

called freely, a task that is not awaited or tracked can run indefinitely in the background, holding resources, writing to databases, or crashing silently long after the code that spawned it has finished.
, if one coroutine raises an exception and
automatically cancelled. They keep running, consuming resources, and their results are simply discarded. With
, exceptions are returned as values and can be completely missed if the caller does not check each result.
If the parent task is cancelled, tasks created with bare
may or may not receive the cancellation depending on how carefully the developer wrote the cleanup code.
fixes all three: tasks cannot outlive their enclosing
block, exceptions are propagated correctly, and cancellation is handled automatically.
block does not exit until every task spawned inside it is done. There is no way for a task to leak out.
If any task raises an unhandled exception, all other tasks in the group are cancelled, and the exception (or a combined
If the enclosing coroutine is cancelled, all tasks in the group receive cancellation automatically.
Code naturally communicates concurrency boundaries — where parallel work starts and where it rejoins.
, you collect results directly from individual
objects, making it easy to know which result belongs to which coroutine.
Code targeting older runtimes cannot use
provide the same semantics for older versions.
When one task fails, all sibling tasks are cancelled. This is the correct default in most cases, but if some tasks should be allowed to finish regardless of sibling failures,
is not the right tool without additional wrapping.
When multiple tasks raise exceptions simultaneously, Python wraps them in an
syntax (also Python 3.11+), which is new to most developers.
All tasks should ideally be spawned from within the
into the same group is possible but requires careful design.
object is created and associated with the current running event loop. Every call to
and schedules it on the event loop, exactly like
would — except the task is now
awaits all registered tasks. The event loop continues running all tasks concurrently during this wait. Several scenarios can then unfold:
Every task completes without raising an exception.
returns normally and execution continues after the block.
The failing task's exception is caught by the group. The group immediately cancels all remaining sibling tasks by calling
on each of them. It then waits for those cancellations to complete (so cleanup code in
blocks runs), and then raises the exception (or an
If more than one task raises an exception (which can happen if tasks fail at nearly the same time before cancellations take effect), all exceptions are collected into an
Scenario D — The parent is cancelled.
receives a cancellation, the group cancels all its tasks, waits for them to finish, and then re-raises the
schedules the coroutine immediately on the event loop. The tasks begin running as soon as the event loop gets control — which happens at the next
block, or when the block exits and

async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
    t1 = tg.create_task(fetch_data("url1"))  # Scheduled now, not yet running
    t2 = tg.create_task(fetch_data("url2"))  # Scheduled now, not yet running
    # Both tasks start running when we hit the next await or exit the block
# Both guaranteed complete here

async with TaskGroup() as tg:
    │
    ├─ tg.create_task(A)  ──► Task A scheduled on event loop
    ├─ tg.create_task(B)  ──► Task B scheduled on event loop
    └─ tg.create_task(C)  ──► Task C scheduled on event loop
    │
    └─► __aexit__() entered
            │
            ├─ Awaits all tasks (A, B, C run concurrently)
            │
            ├─ [All succeed] ──► exits normally
            │
            └─ [Any fails]  ──► cancels siblings
                                 waits for cancellations
                                 raises ExceptionGroup
The key insight is that the scope boundary (
) is also the concurrency boundary. Tasks live inside the scope and cannot escape it.
You have a fixed or dynamically-determined set of concurrent operations that all logically belong together (e.g., fetch data from multiple APIs simultaneously).
You need clean error propagation — one failure should abort the whole group.
You want cancellation to work correctly without writing boilerplate cleanup code.
You are building library code or any code where task leaks would be unacceptable.
tasks that should survive each other's failures. For that, wrap each task's coroutine in a try/except inside a helper coroutine.
— for truly background tasks with no error propagation, use bare
You are on Python < 3.11 and cannot add a dependency on
You need the results in a list and the order matters and you prefer the simpler syntax of

import asyncio

async def fetch(name: str, delay: float) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(delay)
    return f"{name} done"

async def main():
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        task_a = tg.create_task(fetch("A", 1.0))
        task_b = tg.create_task(fetch("B", 2.0))
        task_c = tg.create_task(fetch("C", 0.5))

    # All three completed; results are available via .result()
    print(task_a.result())  # "A done"
    print(task_b.result())  # "B done"
    print(task_c.result())  # "C done"

asyncio.run(main())
The total time is approximately 2.0 seconds (the slowest task), not 3.5 seconds, because all three run concurrently.

import asyncio

async def might_fail(name: str, should_fail: bool):
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
    if should_fail:
        raise ValueError(f"{name} failed")
    return f"{name} succeeded"

async def main():
    try:
        async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
            tg.create_task(might_fail("A", False))
            tg.create_task(might_fail("B", True))   # This will fail
            tg.create_task(might_fail("C", False))
    except* ValueError as eg:
        for exc in eg.exceptions:
            print(f"Caught: {exc}")

asyncio.run(main())
. It matches exceptions of the given type within the group. Each
attribute is a list of all matching exceptions.
Spawning Tasks Dynamically from Within a Running Task
A task running inside the group can spawn more tasks into the same group by capturing a reference to

async def worker(tg: asyncio.TaskGroup, items: list):
    for item in items:
        tg.create_task(process(item))

async def main():
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        tg.create_task(worker(tg, [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]))
    # All process() tasks also guaranteed complete here
This is a powerful pattern for producer-style concurrency where you do not know upfront how many tasks will be needed.

async def main():
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        tasks = [
            tg.create_task(fetch(f"item-{i}", delay=i * 0.1))
            for i in range(5)
        ]

    results = [t.result() for t in tasks]
    print(results)
Because you hold references to individual
objects, results are always associated with their original coroutine — no positional guessing like with
Preventing One Failure from Cancelling Siblings
If you want a task to never bring down its siblings, wrap it in a shield or catch internally:

import asyncio
import aiohttp

async def get_user(session: aiohttp.ClientSession, user_id: int) -> dict:
    async with session.get(f"https://api.example.com/users/{user_id}") as resp:
        return await resp.json()

async def fetch_users(user_ids: list[int]) -> list[dict]:
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
            tasks = [tg.create_task(get_user(session, uid)) for uid in user_ids]
    return [t.result() for t in tasks]
All HTTP requests are made concurrently. If any request raises an unhandled exception, all remaining requests are cancelled cleanly.
Consider a data pipeline for an e-commerce platform that, on each checkout, must simultaneously:
Validate payment with a payment gateway (latency: ~200ms)
Check inventory across three warehouses (latency: ~100ms each)
Fetch the customer's loyalty points balance (latency: ~80ms)
Log the checkout event to an analytics service (latency: ~50ms)
Without concurrency, these steps take roughly 630ms sequentially. With a
, the total latency is approximately the longest single operation (~200ms), cutting checkout response time by 68%.

async def process_checkout(order: Order) -> CheckoutResult:
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        payment_task    = tg.create_task(validate_payment(order.payment))
        inv_task        = tg.create_task(check_all_inventory(order.items))
        loyalty_task    = tg.create_task(get_loyalty_balance(order.customer_id))
        analytics_task  = tg.create_task(log_checkout_event(order))

    payment_result  = payment_task.result()
    inv_result      = inv_task.result()
    loyalty_points  = loyalty_task.result()
    # analytics_task result is not needed

    if not payment_result.approved:
        raise PaymentDeclinedError()
    if not inv_result.all_available:
        raise OutOfStockError(inv_result.missing_items)

    return CheckoutResult(
        confirmation=payment_result.confirmation_id,
        loyalty_points=loyalty_points,
    )
If the payment gateway raises a
, the inventory and loyalty tasks are automatically cancelled — no half-completed state, no orphaned requests continuing to run.
does not cancel siblings on failure by default, does not propagate
cleanly, and returns results in an anonymous list. For any new Python 3.11+ code,

# WRONG — tasks are created outside the group's scope
task1 = asyncio.create_task(coro())
async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
    pass
# task1 is not managed by the group at all
4. Assuming tasks start running immediately on
, not immediately executed. They start running when the event loop gets control — typically at the next
5. Trying to add tasks after the
is called, the group is sealed. Any attempt to call
after the block will raise a
6. Not capturing task references when results are needed.

# Results are lost — no reference to the tasks
async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
    tg.create_task(compute())  # Discarded
Always assign to a variable if you need the result.
for any group of related concurrent operations in Python 3.11+.
Only task-creation code should live there; preprocessing and result handling go before or after.
Wrap side-effecting tasks in try/except inside the coroutine
if you want resilience without cancelling siblings. Never swallow exceptions silently — always log them.
to enforce deadlines on the entire group:

async with asyncio.timeout(5.0):
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        ...
Shielded tasks are not cancelled when the group is cancelled, which can cause exactly the leaks that
with a semaphore to limit concurrency:

sem = asyncio.Semaphore(20)  # At most 20 concurrent tasks

async def limited_fetch(url):
    async with sem:
        return await fetch(url)

async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
    for url in urls:
        tg.create_task(limited_fetch(url))
itself adds negligible overhead — it is a thin wrapper around task scheduling and exception collection. The scalability considerations are entirely in how coroutines are designed.
(HTTP requests, database queries, file I/O) scale very well with
because coroutines yield control during I/O, allowing the event loop to run others. Hundreds to thousands of concurrent I/O tasks are practical on a single thread.
concurrency due to the GIL. For CPU-bound parallel work, combine
to offload to a thread or process pool:

import asyncio
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor

executor = ProcessPoolExecutor()

async def cpu_work(data):
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    return await loop.run_in_executor(executor, heavy_computation, data)

async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
    tasks = [tg.create_task(cpu_work(chunk)) for chunk in data_chunks]
is the main constraint for very high task counts. Each

holds a coroutine frame and associated state — roughly a few kilobytes. At 10,000 concurrent tasks, this amounts to tens of megabytes. Use semaphores to cap concurrency when the number of items is unbounded.
because sibling cancellation happens immediately when a failure is detected, rather than waiting for all tasks to finish before discovering an error.
— the primary subject of this article
— offload blocking calls without breaking structure
backends, and supports Python 3.8+. The de facto standard for library authors who want backend-agnostic structured concurrency.
— the library that pioneered structured concurrency in Python;
was directly inspired by Trio's nurseries. More opinionated, not compatible with native
— async HTTP client/server; pairs naturally with
— async file I/O, commonly used in TaskGroup-based file processing pipelines.
— inspect all currently running tasks to detect leaks.
environment variable — enables extra warnings, including slow callbacks and unawaited coroutines.
names to tracebacks automatically when using
remains useful for quick, simple cases — especially when running a small known set of coroutines and handling each result in order. For anything more complex,
is strictly superior in safety and clarity.
Unlike patterns where exceptions from background tasks require explicit
guarantees that no exception goes unobserved. This is critical for reliability — silent failures in background tasks have historically caused data corruption and inconsistency in async applications.
Without structured concurrency, a loop that freely calls
can spawn thousands of tasks that outlive their intended scope, consuming memory and file descriptors.
ensures all spawned tasks are bounded by the scope's lifetime.
point inside a coroutine. If a task catches and suppresses
without re-raising, it can block the entire group from completing cancellation. The correct pattern is:

async def well_behaved_task():
    try:
        await some_operation()
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        # Cleanup
        await cleanup()
        raise  # Always re-raise CancelledError

with locks or queues. If a task inside a group holds a lock and is waiting on another task in the same group that is also waiting on the lock, a deadlock results. Structure dependency graphs so tasks inside a group do not wait on each other.
enforces structured concurrency: tasks cannot outlive the
Any task failure automatically cancels all sibling tasks in the group, preventing orphaned or zombie tasks.
Multiple simultaneous exceptions are wrapped in an
and must be caught with the

for related concurrent work, and should be the default choice in Python 3.11+.
